title: Joe Wright Bounces Back From His Darkest Hour | The Big Picture (Ep. 37)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Joe Wright about Darkest Hour, recovering from the failure of Pan, and building Churchill with Gary Oldman.

films.title: Darkest Hour
films.note: The new film: one month of Churchill in 1940, pared back to a minimalist chamber piece he scored to Max Richter and Philip Glass.

films.title: Pan
films.note: The studio movie that lost ~$100M and triggered the crisis of confidence he made Darkest Hour to answer.

films.title: Pride and Prejudice
films.note: His debut; his producer warned only one in ten first-timers ever makes a second film.

films.title: Atonement
films.note: Chosen over a Birdsong adaptation on his agent's advice to make the one he felt he knew a secret about; seven Oscar nominations, none for Wright.

films.title: Anna Karenina
films.note: Cited as the ornate aesthetic he wanted to react against and strip back.

films.title: The Soloist
films.note: His earlier true-story film; he refused a false happy ending for Nathaniel Ayers.

films.title: Hanna
films.note: His first film with Saoirse Ronan, whom he first cast at 11 on Atonement.

films.title: Nil by Mouth
films.note: Gary Oldman's directorial film, one of Wright's favorites; his Churchill is the first actor-filmmaker Wright has worked with.

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: Nolan's film covers the same moment; Wright waited to watch it until Darkest Hour was finished and found both had gone minimalist.

films.title: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
films.note: His answer for the last great thing he saw; fascinated by Lanthimos's dissociated performances.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: He calls Greta Gerwig a heroine and a proper filmmaker thinking about form.

The Killing of a Sacred Deer.title: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
